entry.title: Re: Sales order item plant re-determination and pricing r...
entry.content:
You have already handled the plant re‑determination logic in MV45AFZZ/MV45AFZB and built a custom pricing routine (KOBED_XXX). The issue you are seeing — ZTR0 only activating for the first item — is a classic symptom of pricing condition evaluation not being re‑triggered after item‑level changes. Since you don’t want to force full repricing (PRSFD = ‘X’), here are alternative approaches beyond the “read XVBAP‑WERKS instead of VBAP‑WERKS” suggestion:

Explore the below alternative approaches

My recommendations:

  • If you want minimal impact, Option 1 (pricing prepare exit) is the cleanest way to re‑trigger ZTR0.
  • For future‑proof control, Option 2 (field modification) is more aligned with SAP’s pricing framework.
  • Option 3 is a fallback if you need brute‑force certainty that ZTR0 activates for all items.
  •

entry.content:
Getting certified as an SAP Build Developer can feel like a big step.  The content is broad, the expectations are clear, and time is limited.  This session helps you focus on what matters.

Join our 3-hour Partner Learning Accelerator designed for experienced SAP Partners preparing for the SAP Build Developer certification.  We walk through key exam topics across the SAP Build portfolio; with practical guidance you can apply right away.  No fluff.  Just what you need to move forward with confidence.

You will cover application design, data modeling, integrations, process automation, and digital workspaces.  Think of it as a guided walkthrough of the areas that often slow people down, plus tips that come from real project experience.

If you already have hands-on exposure to SAP Build and SAP BTP, this is a strong next step.

- Create Inventory Documents – Cycle Counting
- Count Physical Inventory
I have navigated to the item-level screen in the “Count Physical Inventory” app.
I would like to understand how barcode scanning is supported in this process.
Currently, I do not see any scan button in the app.
My questions:
1. Does the standard app support barcode scanning?
2. If yes, where should the scanning be performed (which field)?
3. Can we use external barcode scanners (keyboard input)?
4. Is custom development (sap.ndc.BarcodeScannerButton) required for camera-based scanning?
Appreciate your guidance. Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Exporting SAP Build Apps: Converting "Web and Mobile"...
entry.content: Export to “Web-only”: There’s no supported way to export a “Web & Mobile” app and have it import as a “Web-only” project in a Build-Default tenant. The project type is embedded in the package and import validation blocks a mismatch (as described in KBA 3693320). Moving your work: The practical, supported approach is to create a new “Web” project in the Build-Default tenant and copy over your UI and logic from the original project. You can copy/paste components and many logic flows across projects, but you must recreate app-level resources (data resources, auth, environment variables) and re-bind where needed.

Shaji

entry.title: Re: Set Posting Periods Automatically
entry.content:
You are correct in your understanding. In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, the Manage Posting Periods app’s automated scheduling works only for normal posting periods. As soon as special periods (used in December for year‑end adjustments) are involved, the recurring job fails with the error you mentioned. This is a standard restriction and SAP deliberately excludes special periods from the automation to avoid unintended postings during sensitive year‑end closing activities.

Explore the below alternatives

entry.title: Re: Desktop Agents in SAP BPA
entry.content: The quickest route is “Pay-As-You-Go for SAP BTP” (PAYG) via SAP Store with the United Kingdom as your billing country. This gives you a commercial SAP BTP global account where you can entitle and use SAP Build Process Automation productively and pay per consumption. If you prefer committed spend and enterprise-style contracting, the “CPEA” (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement) is the alternative, but it requires a sales-led contract and generally takes longer. Once your PAYG global account is provisioned, you can entitle SAP Build Process Automation to a subaccount and deploy the desktop agent for attended/unattended automations.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-2]
entry.content:
Adding CDS Annotations to Build the Fiori UI

In Part 1 , we built and tested our CAP application locally using SQLite. The OData services were working, but when we clicked "Fiori preview" the UI showed raw data with no field labels, no filters, and no meaningful layout.

In this Part, we will add CDS Annotations to shape the Fiori Elements UI —  defining what fields appear, how they are labelled, how the list and detail pages are structured, and which fields get dropdown value helps.

The best part: we do all of this entirely in CDS — no UI5 code needed. Fiori Elements reads these annotations and automatically renders the UI.


WHAT ARE CDS ANNOTATIONS?

CDS Annotations are metadata decorators that you add to your entities or service projections. They tell the Fiori Elements framework how to render the UI — which columns appear in the list, what sections exist on the detail page, which fields should show a value help dropdown, and how labels should read in the browser.

We organize our annotations into three separate files, each focused on one concern:

labels.cds → Human-readable field labels for UI display
layout.cds → Page layout: list columns, detail sections, filters
valuehelps.cds → Dropdown/value help configuration for input fields

Keeping them separate makes the code easier to read and maintain as the application grows.

 

STEP 1 — CREATE THE ANNOTATIONS FOLDER AND FILES

Inside the "app" folder of your CAP project, create a new folder named: annotations

Your folder structure will look like this:

incident-management/
├── app/
│ └── annotations/
│ ├── labels.cds
│ ├── layout.cds
│ └── valuehelps.cds
├── db/
└── srv/

Now create each of the three files with the content below.


a) app/annotations/labels.cds

This file assigns human-readable labels to every field in the Incidents, Customers, Addresses, Status, and Urgency entities. Without these, Fiori Elements falls back to the raw technical field names (e.g., "status_code" instead of "Status").

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../../db/schema';

annotate my.Incidents with @title: 'Incidents' {
    ID           @title          : 'ID';
    createdAt    @title          : 'Created At';
    createdBy    @title          : 'Created By';
    modifiedAt   @title          : 'Modified At';
    modifiedBy   @title          : 'Modified By';
    customer     @title: 'Customer'  : {
        Text           : customer.name,
        TextArrangement: #TextOnly
    };
    title        @title          : 'Title';
    urgency      @title: 'Urgency'   : {
        Text           : urgency.descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    status       @title: 'Status'    : {
        Text           : status.descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    conversation @title          : 'Conversation';
}

annotate my.Customers {
    ID           @title: 'Customer ID';
    createdAt    @title: 'Created At';
    createdBy    @title: 'Created By';
    modifiedAt   @title: 'Modified At';
    modifiedBy   @title: 'Modified By';
    firstName    @title: 'First Name';
    lastName     @title: 'Last Name';
    name         @title: 'Name';
    email        @title: 'Email';
    phone        @title: 'Phone';
    creditCardNo @title: 'Credit Card No';
    incidents    @title: 'Incidents';
    addresses    @title: 'Addresses';
}

annotate my.Addresses with @title: 'Addresses' {
    customer      @title         : 'Customer';
    city          @title         : 'City';
    postCode      @title         : 'Post Code';
    streetAddress @title         : 'Street Address';
}

annotate my.Status {
    code        @title: 'Status Code'  : {
        Text           : descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    criticality @title: 'Criticality';
    descr       @title: 'Status Description';
}

annotate my.Urgency {
    code  @title: 'Urgency Code'  : {
        Text           : descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
}

b) app/annotations/layout.cds
This file defines the full UI layout for the ProcessorService.Incidents entity. It controls both the List Report page and the Object (detail) page.

using {ProcessorService} from '../../srv/incidents_processor';

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents with : {
    HeaderInfo         : {
        TypeName      : 'Incident',
        TypeNamePlural: 'Incidents',
        Title         : {Value: title},
        Description   : {Value: customer.name}
    },
    Facets             : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.CollectionFacet',
            Label : 'Overview',
            Facets: [
                {
                    Label : 'General Information',
                    $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
                    Target: '@UI.FieldGroup#GenInfo',
                },
                {
                    Label : 'Status',
                    $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
                    Target: '@UI.FieldGroup#Status',
                }
            ],

        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            Label : 'Conversations',
            Target: 'conversation/@UI.LineItem',
        }
    ],

    FieldGroup #Status : {
        Label: 'Details',
        $Type: 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            {
                Value                    : status_code,
                Label                    : 'Status',
                Criticality              : status.criticality,
                CriticalityRepresentation: #WithIcon,
            },
            {
                Value                    : urgency_code,
                Label                    : 'Urgency',
                Criticality              : urgency.criticality,
                CriticalityRepresentation: #WithoutIcon,
            }
        ]
    },

    FieldGroup #GenInfo: {
        Label: 'Overview',
        $Type: 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            {Value: customer.name},
            {Value: title}
        ]
    },

    LineItem           : [
        {
            Value         : title,
            .Importance: #High
        },
        {
            Value         : customer.name,
            .Importance: #High,
        },
        {
            Value                    : status_code,
            Criticality              : status.criticality,
            CriticalityRepresentation: #WithIcon,
            .Importance           : #High,
        },
        {
            Value                    : urgency_code,
            .Importance           : #Low,
            Criticality              : urgency.criticality,
            CriticalityRepresentation: #WithoutIcon,
        },
    ],

    SelectionFields    : [
        customer_ID,
        status_code,
        urgency_code
    ],
};

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents.conversation with : {LineItem: [
    {
        Value: message,
        Label: 'Message'
    },
    {Value: author},
    {Value: timestamp}
], }


c) app/annotations/valuehelps.cds

This file adds dropdown / value help popups to input fields on the Incidents entity, making data entry easier and consistent.

using {ProcessorService} from '../../srv/incidents_processor';

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents with {
    status   .ValueListWithFixedValues;
    urgency  .ValueListWithFixedValues;
    customer .ValueList: {
        CollectionPath: 'Customers',
        Parameters    : [
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
                LocalDataProperty: customer_ID,
                ValueListProperty: 'ID'
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'name',
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'email',
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'phone',
            },
        ]
    }
}


STEP 2 — RUN LOCALLY WITH SQLITE AND PREVIEW THE UI

Now that annotations are in place, re-deploy the schema and restart the server:

cds deploy --to sqlite
cds watch

Open your browser at: http://localhost:4004

You will see the familiar CDS welcome page. This time, click "Fiori preview" next to the "Incidents" entity under the /odata/v4/processor service.

 

The List Report and Object page now renders with full layout and labels.

 

 


SUMMARY

In Part 2, we successfully:

✔ Created an "annotations" folder under app/ with three CDS files
✔ Added field labels for all entities (labels.cds)
✔ Defined the List Report columns, filter bar, Object Page layout and detail page sections (layout.cds)
✔ Configured value help dropdowns for Status, Urgency, and Customer (valuehelps.cds)
✔ Previewed the fully rendered Fiori Elements UI locally with SQLite

In Part 3 , we will set up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP — creating the database instance and mapping it to our Cloud Foundry space.

entry.title: Error occurred while executing CKMLCP: The WIP quantities for the material are inconsistent
Re: ECP contract generation from IT0016 + SuccessFactors...
entry.content:
Recommended Approach: Generate labor contracts directly within SuccessFactors Employee Central rather than ECP:

  • Create contract templates within SuccessFactors document management
  • Configure these templates with DocuSign signature capability enabled
  • Use Employee Central data (which can include payroll-relevant information) as the data source
  • Leverage the standard SuccessFactors-DocuSign integration for seamless signature workflows

This approach offers better integration with the existing DocuSign functionality and eliminates the complexity of cross-system data synchronization.

Best Regards

Giorgio

entry.title: Re: I want to restrict the edit functionality after the invoice document posted through workflow.
entry.content: We have observed that after a supplier invoice is approved via workflow and posted, the invoice initiator can still edit the document. Functionally, we want to prevent any further changes once the document has been posted. Requirement: After workflow approval and posting, the invoice document should become non-editable for all users (including the initiator). Any subsequent corrections should only be possible through standard adjustment methods (e.g., credit memo, reversal, or subsequent document), not by direct editing of the posted invoice.

entry.title: Re: The condition class in S4 HANA is not created BAPI_SA...
entry.content:
For a quick fix, enhance your pricing routine (KOBED_XXX) or use USEREXIT_PRICING_PREPARE_TKOMP to force ZCGM evaluation.

For a long‑term solution in Public Cloud, consider moving to the Sales Order OData API (A2X), which is designed to handle mandatory conditions consistently and is SAP’s recommended replacement for the classic BAPI.

entry.title: Re: Bulk upload class set up
entry.content:
Hi,

You can use the class-connector to create hundreds of classes in one action.

  • navigate to download connector template and select 'class connector'
  • dependant on your connector settings select .csv or .txt
  • fill 'uploadsheet' with proper data, don't forget field COL_NUM99_VAL
  • upload file to SF sFTP
  • Run the Class connector and see newly created classes in system.

Test first in staging.

Regards, Bas Adriaans

entry.title: Reducing Structural Friction in SAP FICO to Unlock Real Business Value
entry.content:
FICO is at the center of all SAP financial transactions and most of what a business has to work with to determine their financial status as well as how they are operating effectively and efficiently. So, in a perfect world, it would be very easy to see what is happening within a business from a financial point of view. But, in reality, this does not always happen that way.

Based on what we have seen over time, rarely has SAP FICO not performed as intended. It works, and it works very well and that's why we keep on using it. However, the initial design of the SAP FICO module can be changed by the evolution of the overall system and by numerous decisions made by many different people throughout the years. In this way, the initial structure becomes increasingly complicated, which results in finance teams having greater challenges with the way they are currently able to conduct their daily operational activities.

SAP FICO defines the organization of financial data in a variety of ways. It's not just about keeping transactions but also about having consistency with how those transactions will be organized, controlled and interpreted throughout your organization. When this organization is inconsistent, the ramifications are far reaching and will impact processes, reporting and decision making outside of the system.

In most cases, consistency doesn't happen over night but is gradually built upon over time from various sources. New customizations are made to meet a specific need in the organization, integrations are added as more systems come online and processes are modified to fit the business need. Each of these changes are fine in isolation; however, over time they start to create a level of structural friction that makes things a little more difficult than necessary.

Frictions are easily noticeable in the sense that they start to create sitations nobody really wants to deal with: financial closes take longer to complete, reports need to be manually adjusted prior to being able to rely on them, teams spend more time reconciling the same numbers rather than analyzing them, and when someone needs a concise snapshot of their financials across multiple areas, what should be simple now becomes considerably more complicated.

One project we did with a large energy firm illustrates the same point. We spent time working with their sap systems to organize and combine finance-related data from the many disparate sources of SAP data. Over years of implementation, a large amount of data built up in an uncoordinated manner, and therefore created many inconsistencies across the company.

At first, it appeared that the issue might be due to the data not being available, but that wasn't entirely accurate. Data was in fact present, but in many different formats, extracted by various means, and not at the same level of detail. Many of the departments did not use the same data, nor did they use it consistently.

Related to that, basic analytic activities became very challenging. Every report had to be reformatted and validated, and the time taken to make decisions moved from immediate to much longer due to the effects of the system. Nevertheless, the organization of data within the system created a high level of friction throughout all levels of the company.

In one of our projects, an example of what we found was that operational data coming from the field was fed directly into SAP (the operating system for supply chain management) and automatically caused maintenance and work order processes to be triggered without manual involvement. After establishing the backbone of that integrated process, the end-to-end flow of information could occur without the need for any further validation thereby allowing the process to be completed more quickly and reliably.

It quickly became apparent that this type of friction is very common and that the reason for this kind of friction is generally structural. At its root, we find a human issue since the evolution of systems tends to be a result of different teams making decisions that don’t follow a single, well-thought-out plan.

To ease this friction, you don’t necessarily need to redesign everything from scratchr. In most instances, it would be more beneficial to simplify what already exists. For example, if you align key data structures, eliminate non-essential complexity, and ensure that integrations are based on a consistent logic, they will have a substantially greater positive effect than providing additional tools or layers on top.

SAP FICO is a strong starting point for running finance at present, and the challenge is whether or not it should continue to add on top of it as well as keep the support models for SAP FICO consistent over time.

When the support models are clear, it is easier to trust the data from SAP FICO, and the processes will function smoothly, allowing the teams to make decisions rather than fixing inconsistencies in the system. When they are not, even the simplest tasks will require additional steps, which makes it harder for a user to see value in the system. Our experience shows that growing complexity causes decreased business value, not increased business value.

entry.title: Re: Request to activate scope ID J77
entry.content:
Dear @S0027245434,

The requested scope item is J77 Advanced Bank Account Management this scope activation must be requested via SAP because J77 belongs to the advanced/full cash management scope and requires additional SAP S/4HANA Cloud license coverage

SAP for Me-> Services & Support-> Cases-> Create Case
Use component: XX-S4C-OPR-SRV
Request type/topic: Scope item activation / scope extension
Scope item to be activated: J77 Advanced Bank Account Management

SAP confirms that scope item activation requests for Public Cloud must be reported to the Operations Team under component XX-S4C-OPR-SRV. SAP also confirms that for CBC-managed systems the scope item activation request follows the CBC scope activation process

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003045179

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003122430

IMPORTANT: SAP states that scope activation requires a change project without previous configuration so we should not start configuration in that change project before SAP confirms/executes the scope extension

SAP also provides a scope-item availability/dependency check to confirm whether a scope item is available for activation, whether it requires a SAP case, dependencies, country availability and additional license requirements

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003539934

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Requirment Traciability Matrix
entry.content:
No, SAP SuccessFactors does not provide a specific "Requirement Traceability Matrix" template as a standard product feature for the Discovery Phase.
However, SAP provides several structured approaches and documentation frameworks for requirements gathering and tracking during SuccessFactors implementations, particularly for Employee Central (EC) and Employee Central Payroll (ECP).
In addition, SAP  offers an integrated Toolchain for Project Governance:

  • SAP Signavio – Process intelligence and documentation platform enabling process modeling, gap analysis, and business requirements articulation
  • SAP Cloud ALM – Cloud-native application lifecycle management solution providing end-to-end project delivery governance, issue tracking, and requirements traceability
  • LeanIX – Enterprise architecture management tool supporting requirements alignment with technology portfolios and dependency mapping

Best Regards

Giorgio

entry.title: Building AI Tools and Agents for SAP CX Solutions
entry.content:
This live session provides an introduction into how to design, build, and deploy AI-powered tools and intelligent agents for SAP Customer Experience (CX) solutions using the SAP CX AI Toolkit. Participants will see practical examples of virtual assistants, recommendation engines, and automated agents, and learn how the toolkit accelerates development, integration, and governance across Sales, Service, and Commerce scenarios.

 

Learning Outcome

After participating in this live session, you will understand:

  • Core capabilities of the SAP CX AI Toolkit and how it fits into the SAP CX landscape
  • Typical AI agent types for CX (conversational assistants, recommendation engines, intent classifiers, workflow automations)
  • End-to-end development pattern: data sources → model selection → training/tuning → deployment → monitoring
  • Integration points with SAP CX modules and common extension approaches
  • Key considerations for data privacy, security, explainability, and compliance in CX AI
  • Best practices for governance, testing, and measuring business impact

 

Why Attend a Live Session?

If your current experience in this topic is intermediate and you are willing to have an interactive learning experience, this course is a great fit for you.

 

Register Today

entry.title: Generating Documents via a Link in Custom MDF Objects in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Business Case & Problem Statement

In many SAP SuccessFactors implementations, HR teams rely on a variety of employee-facing documents — offer letters, confirmation letters, salary certificates, experience letters, and more. Traditionally, generating these documents requires navigating multiple screens: locating the employee profile, accessing the Document Generation module, selecting the appropriate template, and downloading the result. This multi-step process is not only time-consuming but also error-prone, especially when the document context is tied to a specific business record stored in a custom Metadata Framework (MDF) object.

The challenge becomes even more pronounced in scenarios where:

  • HR business processes are captured in custom MDF objects (e.g., a probation review record, a training completion record, or a custom agreement tracker).
  • The document to be generated depends on the type of record selected in the MDF object (e.g., different letter types mapped via a picklist).
  • HR administrators want a single-click experience directly from the MDF object record, without switching contexts or re-entering data.

The Solution

SAP SuccessFactors provides a powerful combination of Document Generation, the Metadata Framework, Picklist Center, and Configuration UI that — when wired together correctly — enables HR teams to generate and download a PDF document directly from a link embedded in a custom MDF object screen. No additional navigation. No re-entry of data. One click.

This blog walks through the complete configuration required to achieve this. It is intended for SAP SuccessFactors consultants and HR system administrators who are comfortable working in Admin Center and the Metadata Framework.

Key Business Benefits

  • Reduced Process Time: HR administrators can generate the correct document in a single click directly from the MDF record, eliminating context-switching and manual lookups.
  • Dynamic Template Selection: By linking the picklist external code to the Document Template ID, the system automatically selects the right template based on the document type chosen in the MDF record — no hardcoding required.
  • Scalability: Adding new document types is as simple as adding a new picklist value and a new template — no changes to the MDF object definition or UI configuration are needed.
  • Improved Data Accuracy: Token-based data population pulls live data directly from the system of record, ensuring documents always reflect accurate, up-to-date information without manual intervention.

Solution Architecture Overview

The configuration involves five interdependent components that must be set up in sequence:

  • Document Generation Template — the Word-based template with token placeholders that will be merged into the final PDF.
  • Picklist with External Codes — a picklist whose external code values match the Document Template IDs, acting as the bridge between user selection and template resolution.
  • Custom MDF Object — the business record object with a Picklist field (to select document type) and a String field (to hold the generation link).
  • Configuration UI with a Link — the UI screen for the MDF object, enhanced with a dynamic link element that triggers document generation with the correct token values injected into the URL.
  • Business Rule — an optional but recommended rule to automate link population upon save, ensuring the link is always in sync with the selected document type.

Let’s walk through each step in detail.

Configuration Steps

Step 1: Create Document Generation Template

  • Template Creation Process: Navigate to Admin Center > Document Generation - Manage Document Template and click "Create New"
  • Template Configuration Settings: Select "Document Generation Template" as the category and configure template content with placeholders using [[tokenName]] syntax
  • Document ID Assignment: Assign a unique Document ID to your template - this will be crucial for the picklist configuration in the next step

Step 2: Configure Picklist with External Code

  • Picklist Creation Steps: Go to Admin Center > Picklist Center and create a new picklist for document types
  • External Code Configuration Matching Document ID: Set the external code of your picklist values to match exactly with the Document ID from Step 1 - this creates the connection between your MDF object and the document template

Step 3: Create Custom MDF Object with Required Fields

Field 1: Document Type Field

  • Document Type Field Configuration: In Configure Object Definitions, create a custom MDF object and add a field for document type
  • Picklist Assignment to Document Type Field: Set the data type as "Picklist" and assign the picklist created in Step 2 as the Valid Values Source

Field 2: String Field(Generate Document)

  • String Field Creation: Add a second custom field with data type "String" to capture additional information needed for document generation
  • Field Properties and Data Type Settings: Configure field length, validation rules, and visibility settings as per business requirements

 

Step 4: Create UI and Configure Link with Token Mapping

  • UI Creation Process: Navigate to Admin Center > Manage Configuration UI and create a new UI screen for your custom MDF object
  • Link Addition to UI: Use the "Add Link" functionality in the UI configuration to add a document generation link
  • Token Insertion Methodology: Configure the link URL with tokens that will dynamically populate values from your MDF object fields.

 

Step 5: Create Business Rule with Instance Configuration

Generate Document and Download PDF

  • Document Generation Link Activation: After selecting values in your custom MDF object and saving the record, click on the "Generate Document" link

Summary & Final Thoughts

By combining SAP SuccessFactors’ Document Generation module with a custom MDF object, a Picklist with aligned external codes, a Configuration UI link, and an optional Business Rule, you can deliver a seamless one-click document generation experience to your HR team — all without any external integrations.

The key insight in this configuration is the use of the picklist’s external code as the bridge between the user’s document type selection and the Document Template ID. This elegant mapping makes the solution both flexible and scalable — new document types can be supported simply by adding a new picklist entry and a matching template, with zero changes to the object definition or UI.

This pattern can be applied to a wide range of HR use cases: probation confirmation letters, training completion certificates, employment verification letters, custom agreement forms, and more. Once the framework is in place, rolling out additional document types becomes a configuration-only activity.

Have questions or a variation of this use case? Feel free to share in the comments below. Happy configuring!

entry.title: Business partner Master data change history
entry.content:
Introduction:- 

In modern SAP systems, the concept of the Business Partner (BP) plays a central and combining role in managing master data. SAP introduced the Business Partner approach to simplify data maintenance and improve consistency across applications.  
 
A Business Partner in SAP S/4HANA represents any Person, organization, or group with whom a company conducts business. This includes customers, vendors, employees, and other . SAP puts all roles into one place, reducing duplicates and keeping data accurate. 

One important thing about this Business Partner management is change history tracking. In real-world business scenarios, master data is frequently updated—such as address changes, bank details, or role assignments.   
 
In this blog, we will explore why change history may not appear and what causes it, using simple examples. 

Things You Should Know About Change History in Business Partner:- 

When you need to track changes made to a business partner, you can use the change history feature to view and display those updates clearly. 

We use two tables for change history: CDHDR (Change History Header) and CDPOS (Change History Item). 

In CDHDR, you need to pass the business partner number in the Object ID field, which will display all the related changes. 

In this blog, we will explore the various reasons why change history may not appear in the business partner (BP), using simple and practical examples.

Create a business partner (BP) and make some changes to it. In this example, I have updated the Search Term field from #128 to #127. 

You can check the change history in a business partner (BP) in two ways: 
Extras → Change History → For This Partner: 

This displays all the changes that have been made to the business partner. 
Extras → Change History → For This Field: 

This displays the changes related to a specific field only. 

Extras → Change History → For This Partner: 
This displays the change history as shown below. Since we only updated the Search Term field, it shows changes related to that field only. 

Sometimes, even after making changes, they are not captured in the change history, and no records are stored in the CDHDR or CDPOS tables. 

First, check the BUT000 (General Data of Business Partner) table for the field where changes are not being recorded. Then, verify whether the “Change Document” option is enabled in the data element, as shown below. Make sure this option is active so that change history can be stored properly. 

Second, you need to check the field name in the BP transaction using F1 help and then go to Technical Information. This helps you identify the correct technical field details. 

Then, verify the corresponding field name in the BUT000 table. 

Then, check the field group for that field. Click on the field name and, in the T-code search bar, enter BDT_ANALYZER and press enter . 

Then identify the field group for NAME_ORG1 as follows: 
Sections → BUP010 - Name → BUP200 - Organization → Field Groups → 0045 - Organization: Name 1 
So, the field group for NAME_ORG1 is 0045. 

Now go to transaction BUS11 and check whether field group 45 is maintained for NAME_ORG1. This ensures that the field can be fetched through the BAPI and displayed in the output.
 
Sometimes, the field is not maintained in the BUS11 transaction, and in such cases, the change history will not be retrieved for that field, so it will not appear in the output. 

Thirdly, go to transaction BUS7 (Display View "Events": Overview). 

  • Select the CHGD1 event and navigate to Events → Function Module Allocation to view the assigned function modules.  
  • For function modules BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD1 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD1 (Applications BUA and BUB), ensure that the “Call” column is marked with ‘X’.  
  • Navigate back (F3).  

Repeat the same steps for the events CHGD2, CHGD3, and CHGD4, and verify the corresponding function modules: 

  • CHGD2 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD2 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD2  
  • CHGD3 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD3 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD3  
  • CHGD4 → BUA_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD4 and BUB_BUPA_EVENT_CHGD4  
  • Finally, save the changes on the screen . 

We have the report BUSCHDOC to view the change history for multiple business partners. Using this report, we can display change history for a large number of BPs efficiently. 

Additionally, the BPCD transaction is available for the BUSCHDOC report. It generates the selection screen based on the application object and provides a multi-screen report to analyze business partner change documents. 

Prerequisites to execute BUSCHDOC report:- 
Sometimes in customer systems, the BUSCHDOC report may not display the output screen. To make the output visible, you need to ensure the following: 

BUPSELG5 and BUPSELG0 reports are used to generate the select options for the reports BUSCHDOC for a given application object (Eg: BUPA). 

Execution of these reports is a prerequisite for execution of BUSCHDOC  reports. First report BUPSELG5 should be executed then BUPSELG0. 

Please note these reports are language specific and must be executed once for each logon language. 

Many times, we may encounter a “TIME_OUT” dump while executing this report, especially when processing millions of business partners to display change history. 

Resolution:- 
Business Partner is the leading object in S/4 systems, and the number of applications consuming BP has been increasing with each service pack release. To determine BP changes, all registered applications must be called, and their responses consolidated to generate the output. As the number of applications grows, the processing time also increases. 

Since running BUSCHDOC for all BPs is not a regular daily activity, it is recommended to split the range into variants and execute the report as a background job. 

The second approach is to split the process into parallel jobs instead of running a single job with multiple variants, which can help reduce the overall execution time. However, in this case, you will need to consolidate the outputs from all job logs. 

Simple example to use BUSCHDOC report:- 
This is the BUSCHDOC report output screen. Here, we need to enter BUPA as the application object. 

While executing the BUSCHDOC report in the foreground, it is mandatory to enter the Business Partner number. 

Since entering the Business Partner number is mandatory, we are passing 154-12071. 

If no Business Partner number is entered, the system will display the following popup. Therefore, it is important to always provide a BP range to avoid a runtime dump. 

Output: Change history for BP 154-12071.

Conclusion:- 
In my project, validating SAP Business Partner change document settings improved data transparency and helped quickly identify missing logs using BUS7, BUS11, and BUSCHDOC (BPCD). This reduced troubleshooting effort and sped up issue resolution.

Handling performance with background runs and BP ranges avoided timeouts and ensured smooth processing. Overall, it made monitoring and support more efficient.

If you’ve encountered similar situations or have different approaches, feel free to share your thoughts or ask questions in the comments!

entry.title: PLMSI Version 3.0 & all FP– Guideline for Logging and Monitoring for On-Premises and Private Cloud
entry.content:
This guide is intended to provide an overview of the PLM system integration (PLMSI) monitoring capabilities. It also covers approaches to error handling and troubleshooting. For more in-depth details, please refer to the official SAP documentation.
PLMSI for S/4HANA Administration Guide

Why Logging and Monitoring for PLM system Integration is so important?
Logging and monitoring are critical for PLM system integration (PLMSI), because integrations are distributed, can be asynchronous, and business critical.
Efficient and reliable process monitoring through simplicity, traceability, clear responsibilities for key business users brings the advantage for a working system and a clear understanding where the possible problem between an external PLM system connected to PLMSI lies.
When something goes wrong, logs and monitoring are often the only reliable way to understand what happened.

Content:

  1. Prerequisites
  2. Logging and Monitoring
    • SAP Application Log
    • SAP Application Interface Framework
    • PLMSI Workbench
  3. Error Handling and Troubleshooting and recommended actions
    • Possible status codes and errors from PLMSI for SAP S/4HANA
    • Troubleshooting with analyzing request payload

Note: PLM system integration 3.0 is delivered as add-on to SAP S/4HANA on-premises and SAP S/4HANA Cloud and Private Cloud Edition. 

1. Prerequisites

  • PLMSI add-on or S/4HANA private or on-prem 3.0 onwards Support Packages is installed.
    Please find more information about this on the SAP documentation.
  • PLMSI Configuration and external configuration is done, connection is set up between an external PLM system and PLMSI.

Users need authorization through authorization object /PLMF/ADM and general administration rights. SAP documentation: PLMSI for S/4HANA Authorization concept overview

2. Logging and Monitoring
Tip: Always start troubleshooting with logs and monitoring tools before changing configuration or code.

2.1 SAP Application Log

  • The application log allows an application or object oriented recording of events. Both are classified by object and any subobject which belong to it.
  • The application log helps to identify business and technical errors that occur during data transfer. In case of an error it gives first indications what the error cause and source might be.
  • The default expiration date for PLMSI log objects is 90 days after the creation date.

Using the SAP Application Log
1. Open the SAP Application Log via the transaction /SLG1.



2. Choose from the available log objects or search for all with /PLMF*.


You can use the F4 help to review and select from the available sub objects.

System view:
The system shows all log messages that are relevant for this aspect of the integration with the external PLM system.

For more information about SAP Application Log refer to the SAP Help documentation:
PLMSI for S/4HANA Administration SAP Application Log

2.2 SAP Application Interface Framework

  • The SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) enables monitoring the endpoints of the integration in a single framework residing in the SAP backend system. The AIF is a more advanced monitoring tool to also assess what data was passed and generated by the integration. It is possible to see the in- and outbound payload within AIF.
  • Interface Names allow a more specific analysis based on the entity and call type.

Prerequisites:

  • AIF is implemented and error log level is set to FULL.
  • PLMSI Business Configuration Sets (BC Sets)  for AIF are implemented.
  • AIF Logging in transaction /SPRO IMG customizing for PLMSI in Define Scenario and Version is set to active in the tested Scenario.

Using the SAP Application Interface Framework

  1. Open transaction /AIF/ERR and select the namespace /PLMSI, an interface name if necessary, and the date range.
  2. Select either only the “error logs” or “all logs”.

     


    System view:

    The system shows all log messages configured for this aspect of the integration that are relevant to the external PLM system, broken down by individual entities that can be sent and each separated into Maintain and Read requests.

    For more information about AIF refer to the SAP Help documentation:
    SAP Application Interface Framework
    PLMSI for S/4HANA Administration AIF

2.3 PLMSI Workbench

  • The PLM System Integration Workbench is designed for technical development support use cases. As a power user or administrator, you can use the PLMSI Workbench report to work on PLMSI messages.
  • Search for inbound and outbound messages that are processed by PLMSI and take certain actions for a message or object, for example, copy a message, download request, response, and system data, or activate break point IDs used in PLMSI.

 Prerequisites for extensive search :

  • PLMSI add-on Version 3.0 FP 02 or onwards Support Packages for S/4HANA private cloud or on-prem is installed.
  • SLG1 is logging works.
  • Users need authorization through authorization object /PLMF/ADM.

Using the PLMF Workbench

  • Eather go to transaction /PLMF/WORKBENCH.
  • Or call up PLMSI Workbench from the SAP Easy Access- SAP Menu Cross-Application Components - PLM System Integration - PLMSI Workbench.
  • Fill in the parameters you want to search for.

System view:
The system shows all log messages configured for this aspect of the integration that are relevant to the external PLM system, depending on the search terms.
Additionally the Object Details, send JSON Request and Response if available are also visible. For more information about SAP PLMSI Workbench visit the SAP Help documentation:
PLMSI for S/4HANA Administration PLMSI Workbench

3. Error Handling and Troubleshooting
General error handling troubleshooting overview for external PLM to SAP S/4 Hana System. Find more information about this topic in the following SAP documentation:
PLMSI for S/4HANA Administration Operating PLMSI

Prerequisite:
Check SLG1 Logs, PLMSI Workbench or Activate AIF Logging and define API Msg Type in Define Scenario and Version.

General approach:

  1. Check status code. In case of application errors (201) proceed with below mentioned steps.
  2. Check if object was created with transferred data.
  3. Check SLG1.
  4. Check AIF Monitoring / external PLM logs.
  5. Check PLMSI Workbench.
  6. Check if configuration matches your expectation.
    • SAP PLMSI IMG Customizing.
    • Ext. PLM configuration.

3.1 Possible status codes and errors from SAP:
Here are some examples of possible status codes and errors from PLMSI for SAP S/4HANA and recommended actions.


Status code

Information

Payload persisted

Recommended actions

201 - error / success

 

Application error:
The request was processed, but an application-level error occurred.

 

Yes

 

· Review monitoring and logging tools to identify the root cause:

  • SLG1 – Check application logs for detailed error messages
  • AIF (Application Interface Framework) – Verify message status and payload processing
  • External PLM Logs – Cross-check the originating system for inconsistencies
  • PLMSI Workbench – Validate message flow and processing status

· Confirm whether the payload was persisted correctly and can be reprocessed if needed

 

400 - bad request

 

Technical error:
The request could not be processed due to invalid or incomplete input.

 

No

 

· Verify that all mandatory fields are provided

· Check field constraints:

  • Maximum field length is not exceeded
  • Correct data types are used (e.g. EDM.String)

· Validate the request structure and format (JSON/XML)

· Ensure the business scenario can be correctly determined from the payload

 

401 - not authorized

 

Technical error:
Authentication failed when accessing the SAP Web Application Server.

 

No

· Verify technical user credentials:

  • Correct username
  • Valid password

· Ensure the SAP user:

  • Exists in the target system
  • Is of type System (or required technical user type)

· Confirm host and port configuration maintained in external PLM

· Check that the required authorization roles are assigned to the user

 

403 - forbidden

 

Technical error:
The request was authenticated but blocked due to missing permissions or security restrictions.

 

No

· Check network and infrastructure settings:

  • Firewall rules allow the connection

· Validate user authorizations:

  • Required roles and permissions are assigned

· Verify authentication requirements:

  • SSL certificate is valid, trusted, and correctly configured

· Review SAP security logs for authorization check failures

 

404 - not found

 

Technical error:
The requested resource or service endpoint could not be located.

 

No

 

· Verify that the SAP service is:

  • Active
  • Properly deployed

· Check whether extPLM is calling the correct endpoint:

  • URL path
  • Service name
  • HTTP method

· Confirm system aliases and routing configuration

 

500 - internal server error

 

Technical error:
A technical error occurred on the server while processing the request. The payload was not persisted.

 

No

 

· Investigate server-side runtime issues:

  • Check for ABAP dumps or unhandled exceptions (e.g. via ST22)

· Validate the request payload handling:

  • Ensure the referenced JSON file is reachable and syntactically correct

· Verify service availability:

  • Confirm the HTTP service is registered and active in the SAP system

·  Check security token handling:

  • Validate CSRF token handling and ensure the token is fetched and passed correctly

· Review application and system logs:

  • Analyze error details in SAP logs to identify the exact failure point

 

3.2 Troubleshooting with analyzing request payload:

  • Most errors occurred due to wrong values sent within the request payload. These values are either incorrectly extracted within external PLM system or not configured in SAP PLMSI. This leads either to an unexpected behavior in the target system or to an application error.
  • It is recommended to validate the request payload at first if the error messages from the target system are unclear. SAP saves both, the request and response payload.

General approach:
1. Go to Transaction SLG1 and copy the External Message ID from the Error that you want to investigate.

2. Or go to AIF and copy Message ID for Internal Message ID.

3. Go to PLMSI WORKBENCH and enter the message ID or fill in the parameters you want to search for.

Note: It is possible to search in PLMF Workbench without an Internal Message ID and External Message ID. Therefore, you can search for example by scenario with date and time range and additional if preferred with a Domain Model Object Type.

For more information about Error Handling and Troubleshooting PLMSI for SAP S/4HANA, see the official SAP Help documentation:
PLMSI for S/4HANA Administration Error Handling
PLMSI for S/4HANA Administration Troubleshooting

For Troubleshooting and recreation of an error case in SAP without external PLM system, please visit the following documentation:
PLMSI for S/4HANA How to test the integration without a external PLM system

entry.title: Re: SAP CI to ABAP Integration: Seamless XML to Internal Table Mapping
entry.content:
If this is the case can you please add an option into your list as a common approach? Appreciate it.

There are two common approaches:

  1. Use XSLT mapping in SAP Backend (SAP ECC/S4HANA) to transform the XML into the required structure. ( You can follow blog https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-blog-posts/xslt-transformations-... )
  2. Prepare the XML in SAP CI in ABAP XML format so that it can be directly converted into an internal table using ABAP transformation.

entry.title: Initial Stock Wrong Price Change -> "Allow Open Status for Previous Period" in CKMLCP
entry.content:
In an implementation project in S4HC Public, with actual costing, we have done in month X-1 the initial stock update, quantity * initial price of material master (Inventory Account BSX to Intial Stock Charge Account BSA). Then the client realized that the prices were wrong, so we did an MR21 with the stocked already charged (BSX to Price Difference Account UMB). Later I realized that the correct way should have been reversing the initial stock, changing the price and then post the initial stock again. Now, at the end of month X-1, we have Inventory Account balance against both Price Difference Account and Initial Stock Charge Account (not only Initial Stock Charge Account as it should be). Executing CKMLCP for X-1 clears the Price Difference Account to 0 but Inventory account goes back to initial price (because of PUP), so this is not a solution.
We came up with this only solution:
- Doing a manual posting to clear Price Difference Account against the Initial Stock Charge Account, so that Inventory Account BSX matches Initial Stock Charge Account. Even though we cannot do this posting at the level of material/plant, this will leave accounting with the correct accounts for month X-1 (Initial Stock of month X)
- Not executing CKMLCP for X-1 and executing it for the first month of the go-live (month X) activating the flag "Allow Open Status for Previous Period" in Preparation step Parameters of CKMLCP. This way, the Price Differences are not carried forward to month X (they are cleared with the manual posting mentioned above), the initial stock of month X is the correct one for all materials (with the new price after the MR21) and only the price differences that appear on month X will be used for the month X Material Ledger Closing.

Do you think this approach is the optimal one given the mistake of doing MR21 with stock already charged? Will activating the flag "Allow Open Status for Previous Period" work for Month X Material Ledger Closing? (I have done a test in DEV system and it allowed me to execute with no problem at all with previous opened periods.

entry.title: Choosing the Right TMS SAP S/4HANA: Why one Size Rarely Fits all
entry.content:
Recently, I was approached by the supply chain leadership team and the head of IT of one of the top European giants to shortlist five transportation planning solutions. The company was midway through an SAP S/4HANA migration. Their legacy TMS was to be replaced for expiring software and support issues. Every planning vendor was telling them that they can integrate seamlessly with S/4HANA. Although they all integrate, that does not mean it is the right fit.

The chemical company had freight mix like most shippers I worked with in the past, having full truckloads, less than truckloads, and small parcels. They moved FTLs between DCs and major customers. The used LTL carriers for regional replenishment to smaller retail locations. And they shipped thousands of small parcels daily direct to consumers, parts for warranty replacement, or samples through UPS, FedEx, and regional carriers. So fundamentally, three transportation planning problems. They needed one technology to address all of them within an S/4HANA environment.

After working for 18 years with multiple industries implementing SAP Transportation Management and Order to Cash processes across manufacturing, retail, and third-party logistics, I have learned that the biggest mistake companies make in TMS selection is treating it as a single problem. It is rarely one; FTL planning, LTL optimization, and small parcel management, each one by itself, are unique opportunities. Generally, one software decision for all freight modes is often not right.

Every transportation planning stack relies on a central decision-making layer that drives optimization, execution, and responsiveness. The system that holds the optimization logic, the carrier master, the lanes, the rates, and the planning rules. When you run SAP S/4HANA, you have a choice to choose if you want the decision-making inside the SAP ecosystem or if you want it in a best-of-breed external TMS that connects to S/4HANA as its backbone.

This is not a technical question. It is a strategic one. The answer shapes every other decision.

If the decision-making lives in SAP, specifically in SAP TM, which is embedded in S/4HANA, you get native data flows, a single master data model, tight integration between order management, inventory, realistic ATP, and transportation, and no middleware layer to maintain between your planning system and your ERP. The trade-off is generally SAP TM’s optimization capabilities, while robust, are not specialized like some best-of-breed tools that offer transportation-specific reporting, parcel optimization, and overall market freight rate intelligence (highs and lows).

If the decision-making lives in a best-of-breed TMS system a platform like Blue Yonder, MercuryGate, Oracle TMS, or Uber Logistics they typically get more sophisticated optimization engines for specific modes or network configurations as a vendor whose product specifically focuses on transportation. The trade-off is integration complexity, duplicate master data, and the ongoing cost and maintenance support for the connection between your TMS and S/4HANA.

The company was leaning towards best of breed. By the end of the evaluation, they chose SAP TM for their FTL and LTL operations and a dedicated parcel management solution for their small parcel volume integrated into S/4HANA through an API.

 

The three freight mixes are three different decisions.

Full truckload (FTL) planning is fundamentally about lane selection, load optimization, and carrier selection against the freight contracts. The key variables are weight, cube, origin-destination pairs, and service level commitments. SAP TM was designed around this problem and handles it in depth with multi-leg chains, intermodal transfers, freight tendering, and freight order management. For FTL networks of any complexity, SAP TM is a strong native choice within S/4HANA.

Less than truckload (LTL) is a different optimization challenge, as you are not moving a dedicated truck from original to destination; rather, you are sharing trailer space with other shippers, which means your cost and transit time depend on carrier networks. The freight classification by National Motor Freight classification code, dimensions, and accessorial charges that vary by carrier in ways that are difficult to model cleanly. LTL rating is complex because of deficit weight calculation, weight breaks, base rates, discount structure, FAK (Freight All Kinds Agreements) for specific product freight classes, and accessorials like liftgate, inside delivery, and other surcharges interact differently across every carrier.

SAP TM can manage LTL freight orders and carrier selection, but its native LTL rating engine has significant configuration effort to replicate the full complexity of real-world LTL tariff structures. Several clients I have worked with have handled this by integrating a specialized LTL rating API or Idocs with tools like SMC3’s RateWare or a third-party rating engine to feed accurate LTL rates into SAP TM's carrier selection logic, while keeping the freight order and execution management inside SAP TM. This hybrid approach generally preserves the S/4HANA integration benefit while closing the LTL rating gap.

Small Parcel operates under a totally different set of rules. Parcel carriers UPS, FedEx, USPS, DHL, and other regional operators operate on a published rate, dimensional weight, zone pricing, residential and fuel surcharges, and service tiers that vary by carrier. So, the main challenge is not lane planning like it is in freight. It's choosing the right carrier and service for each individual shipment, often at very high volume and in real time.

SAP TM is not really built to compete with specialized parcel platforms in that space. Tools like ShipERP, XPS ship, or EasyPost are made specifically for carrier connectivity, live rate shopping, label printing, and tracking. If a company ships a few hundred parcels or more each day, the better approach is usually a dedicated parcel platform connected to S/4HANA, so S/4HANA can still manage the order, cost, and tracking date without trying to do everything itself.

The real question is not which system should handle every mode. It is how to connect the right tool for each shipping mode back to S/4HANA as the main source of truth for cost, delivery, and customer communication.

For my client, that changed the whole evaluation. Instead of trying to force one TMS to cover everything, we designed a layered setup. SAP TM for FTL and managed LTL, a parcel platform for small parcels, and a clean integration model that kept S/4HANA as the central record for delivery and freight cost.

Selecting a transportation management solution for an S/4HANA environment is not just about vendor demos or license price. There are various factors to know, starting from how well the system can fit the business processes, how it integrates with S/4HANA, and whether the users can actually adopt and trust it.

Integration: A TMS system that can only exchange the data with S/4HANA in batches is very different from one that works in real time. In transportation, especially for outbound orders and freight settlement, delayed or unclear integration often can lead to manual work, data mismatches, and rework. Changes in demand documents are another aspect of planning, with the source of truth being the ERP system and freight cost record that stay aligned across finance and logistics.

 

Planning: FTL fits well with SAP TM, while LTL depends heavily on rating accuracy and carrier tariff logic. A small parcel is different from both as it requires real-time rate shopping, service level, label generation, and carrier API connectivity at very high speed. The key is to match the right tool to the mode instead of choosing one system to solve for all equally.

 

Carrier Connectivity: Some best-of-breed platforms have strong carrier networks and can connect more easily to booking, tracking, and invoicing processes. A real advantage for companies who have frequent carrier onboarding needs. SAP’s Business Network for Logistics capabilities have improved, but some specialist platforms offer broader carrier reach in some markets.

User Adoption: Transportation planners work under huge time constraints at times, handling exceptions and needing quick turnaround; they need a system that is intuitive and reliable. If the user interface is too complex, planners will build workarounds in spreadsheets and phone calls, which will defeat the purpose of the whole solution. End-user testing being part of the evaluation is not always a decision for later.

The best transportation solution is one that matches the freight complexity, integrates cleanly with S/4HANA, manages master data well, keeps total cost under control, and is actually usable by the planners who run the business every day.

After the evaluation, the client chose a layered solution instead of trying to use one system for everything. This decision helped the freight network that was too mixed and complex in a single platform approach.

For FTL and international multi-modal freight, they selected SAP TM embedded in S/4HANA. This made sense because their network included ocean and air freight across 14 countries, multi-leg chains, and customs milestones. SAP TM fit that complexity well, and since the master data governance in S/4HANA was already strong, they did not want to add the additional burden of managing a separate transportation system.

For LTL, they kept SAP TM as the main execution platform but connected it to a third-party LTL rating engine through an API. This gave them more accurate carrier-specific pricing and better handling of accessorials while still keeping the S/4HANA delivery document as the main record. The integration worked well because the rating engine acted like a service rather than trying to own the planning process itself.

For small parcels, they chose a dedicated parcel management solution and connected to S/HANA using outbound delivery data. This platform handled real-time rate shopping across multiple carriers and label generation and carrier-specific compliance needs as well. Shipment cost and tracking information flowed back into S/HANA so that finance and customer service still have visibility and also transmit to end customers in the system.

One of the vendors claimed it could cover all three modes, but the proof of concept showed the LTL rating logic could not handle it without manual interference and was easy to knock down.

This is not a recommendation for every company. An organization with simple domestic FTL and little or no parcel volume might do just fine with one good TMS. But the right choice always depends on the company’s freight mix, how complex the network is, how much IT support is available, and how solid the master data is.

The main idea is simple: Use SAP TM where it fits best, use specialized tools where they bring real value, and keep S/4HANA as the core system that ties everything together.

entry.title: SAP EWM - Custom WOCR for Optimizing Physical Inventory Printing
entry.content:
Business Case: 

During Physical Inventory operations, warehouse teams are required to count materials stored across multiple storage types. Once a Physical Inventory (PI) document is created and activated, the next critical step is printing the count sheets for ground-level staff to begin their counting activity.

However, with thousands of line items involved, SAP's print processing takes approximately 1 to 1.5 hour (Considering various factors) — a significant bottleneck that delays the entire inventory counting process. This wait time directly impacts operational efficiency, as the ground team cannot commence their activity until the printouts are in hand.

To address this challenge, a targeted solution can be implemented that can streamline the print process, dramatically reducing the time required to generate inventory documents. This improvement enables warehouse teams to begin their counting activities much sooner, resulting in faster inventory cycles and reduced operational downtime.

A) Configuration: 

Warehouse Order Creation Rule

SCM EWM > EWM > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order >

i) Define Sort Rules for Warehouse Tasks 

Define Sort Rules

 Define Sort Fields

ii) Define Limit Values for the Size of a Warehouse Order

Note: Limit created for demonstration. 

Note: This is the actual Limit which is used.

iii) Define Creation Rules for Warehouse Orders

Determine WOCR

iv) Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

B) Master Data 

T code: /N/SCWM/GCMC

Application: PPI                   Maintenance Group: PI

C) Testing

1) /N/SCWM/PI_CREATE  Create Physical Inventory document

Selected AA, activated document and Flag is set. Click on Save and it will create a PI document.

2) Go to T code: /N/SCWM/MON

Multiple warehouse orders are created are per creation rule.  

3) Go to T code: /N/SCWM/PRPI_DOC  to print give your selection criteria 

4) Go to T code: SP01 To check Spool. 

 

Conclusion: By leveraging optimized Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR) and defining strategic limit values, we’ve demonstrated that it is possible to bypass the wait time. This solution ensures that the ground team moves from "waiting" to "counting" almost instantly, maximizing labor utilization and ensuring that the physical inventory process remains a lean, high-speed operation rather than an administrative hurdle.

entry.title: Re: Solution Manager rises an MAXDB Log fill level error
entry.content:
Hello Alessandro,

AI was right ;0)

The reason for alert is, it was concerning that having log filling at 1.5*LogSegmentSize your system is at danger, because normally the log is filled about LogSegment level and afterwards ideally it must be saved with the log backup. If autolog backup is activated in your system, it will always trigger after filling of the LogSegment size on the log volume. So alert is concerning about that either autolog backup is out of function or you backup infrastructure is too slow. Getting LOG FULL situation means stand still.

However in many customer environments we could see, that log segment size compared to the size of the log volume is too low, therefore reaching the log volume filling of 1.5 of log segment size means no danger. But alerting is hard coding and hard meaning.

The log segment size is defined by the MaxDB parameter AutologBackupSize and expressed in the 8k pages. If it is too low and you are saving the backups not by autolog , but for example timely scheduled, it can easy happen that you reach 1.5 of log segment size filling. If the sum size of the log volumes is much higher as AutoLogBackupSize, in this case you can probably increase the AutologBackupSize parameter. However if you run log backups with autolog, it will increase the size of log backups and also increase the time gap between auto log backups, what impact the availability of data in case of restore.

If you schedule log backups and not use autolog backup, the AutoLogBackupSize will have only ´CCMS alerting effect.

Regards, Alexander

entry.title: Grouping WBS issue during MRP Run
entry.content:
Dear Experts,
 
I am writing to seek your guidance regarding Grouping WBS MRP issue we are experiencing.
 
We have created a Project WBS hierarchy consisting of a grouping WBS, [WBS-Project-XXXX], with three lower-level WBS elements: [WBS1-Project-XXXX], [WBS2-Project-XXXX], and [WBS3-Project-XXXX]. Network activities have been created for these lower-level elements, with finished goods and parameter effectivity (Model Unit values) assigned as follows:
 
- [WBS1-Project-XXXX]: FG Material [XXX], Model [XXXX], Unit 1
- [WBS2-Project-XXXX]: FG Material [XXX], Model [XXXX], Unit 2
- [WBS3-Project-XXXX]: FG Material [XXX], Model [XXXX], Unit 3
 
Our requirement is for the MRP run to generate planned orders for in-house production materials using the respective lower-level WBS, while generating purchase requisitions for externally procured materials using the grouping WBS.
 
Currently, we have observed that while purchase requisitions are correctly following the grouping WBS, the planned orders for in-house production are also being assigned to the grouping WBS instead of the intended lower-level WBS.
 
Could you please provide guidance on how to ensure that planned orders are correctly created with lower-level WBS elements? We anticipate that this resolution will also facilitate the issuance of component stock (movement type 261) from the grouping WBS to production orders at the lower level without errors.
 
Our SAP Version: S/4HANA 2023 FPS03 (On-Premise).
 
Thanks & Regards,
Ramasamy Vasantha Kumar.

entry.title: SAP Integrated Product Development What's New: Update 03/2026
entry.content:
What’s New for SAP Integrated Product Development: March 2026

Welcome to the March 2026 blog! This update introduces powerful enhancements designed to accelerate innovation, strengthen collaboration, and improve efficiency across the product lifecycle. With expanded engineering capabilities and deeper integration with SAP and partner solutions, it further enhances how teams work together—from concept through manufacturing and beyond.

Collaboration

Status–Based Workflow Automation in Configure Collaborations App

As a Business Administrator, you can now configure status-based workflow assignments that enable the system to automatically trigger predefined workflows when a collaboration reaches a specific status.

For example, when a collaboration is marked as Completed, the system can automatically initiate the workflow “Notify All Collaboration Administrators.”

The Configure Collaborations app now includes a dedicated section called Status–Workflow Automation. This feature allows you to seamlessly map collaboration statuses to corresponding workflows.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Configure Collaboration.

Product Data Integration

Design Items Explorer App Enhancement

The Design Items Explorer app has been enhanced to provide greater flexibility and usability. Users can now:

  • Assign an existing product to a design item that does not yet have a linked product
  • Download design item files directly by clicking on the file name link

These updates streamline your workflow and reduce manual steps — available now.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Details View. 

 Product Data Integration

Structure Relationships

The Design Items Explorer app now provides enhanced visibility into the structure relationships between design items:

  • Child Relationships: Display dependencies on other design items required to load the design item in the authoring system
  • Parent Relationships: Show how the design item is used across other design item

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Details View. 

Specification Management

Alternative Units of Measure for specifications

 When the Base UoM and Alternative UoM option is enabled in the Specification Types app, the system allows you to maintain multiple units of measure for a specification within the specification created using that specification type in the Manage Specifications application.

As a result:

  • The Base Unit of Measure field becomes available
  • The Alternative Unit of Measure table is displayed

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Managing Alternative UoMs for Specifications. 

Comparison of composition based on real values

Previously comparison of composition in the main view of the Compare Specifications app was based on item numbers. Now the comparison is based on the actual composition values instead.

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see Comparing Specifications. 

 Visualization

New cross-section settings in Viewer

You can now access enhanced cross-section controls directly from the Viewer toolbar. A new Settings option in the Cross Section dropdown allows you to customize how cross sections are displayed.

With this enhancement, you can:

  • Show or hide the outline and capping
  • Customize their colors
  • Adjust the outline width
  • Control the visibility of the editing user interface

[Shows an overview of the changes and UI, as outlined in the text.]

To learn more about this feature, see User Interface Overview - Viewer. 

More Information / Additional Links 

Also, check out our What’s New document for regular updates!

To learn more about the features of SAP Integrated Product Development, visit the SAP Supply Chain Management Enablement Hub.

You are invited to join the  SAP PLM Experts Forum  group on LinkedIn.

By the way, do you know how to provide feedback for SAP Integrated Product Development? Watch this video to find out how and send your feedback directly from the application. We always look forward to hearing from you.

entry.title: Sapphire Orlando 2026: Responsible innovation - Joule architecture for security and governance
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

SAP Sapphire & ASUG Annual Conference Orlando 2026 is where SAP will showcase its latest AI advancements, including the next evolution of Joule. Today, I want to share insights about my session on Responsible AI —and why it matters now more than ever.

About Me
I’m part of the SAP Business AI team, where I lead work on Responsible AI. In simple terms, Responsible AI is about asking (and answering) the uncomfortable questions: What can go wrong with AI? How might it be misused? Where do we need stronger safeguards —across ethics, cybersecurity, and compliance? How to verify safety and security of AI applications? That includes topics like AI security, guidance on ethical usage, and navigating regulatory expectations—whether they’re country-specific or industry-specific. My role is to bring together experts across ethics, security, and compliance so we can build Business AI that earns trust by design.

My Session at SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026
Session title: Responsible innovation: Joule architecture for security and governance
Session link

AI is a core part of SAP’s strategy, and Sapphire is where you’ll see new innovations and experiences in Joule. Alongside that innovation, we will also talk about responsibility: how we design Joule and agentic applications so they can be used safely, governed appropriately, and protected against misuse.
Specifically, I’ll address:
- The architectural building blocks of Joule
- How they reduce common risk patterns
- How governance controls apply when AI agents are involved behind the scenes

Agent governance in business systems is not a nice-to-have feature—it is a mandatory requirement to ensure the safety of businesses and their users. We will discuss how we solve this challenge in Joule, and I look forward to learning from your experiences and ideas.

Thanks,
Sudhakar

entry.title: SAP PM Maintenance Plans – Compliance KPI Challenges for Equipment Under NR13 Inspection
entry.content:
Hello SAP PM / Asset Management community,

I would like to raise a question regarding SAP PM Maintenance Plans and regulatory compliance management.

Here in Brazil, we have a regulatory requirement called NR13, which governs the integrity management of static equipment such as pressure vessels, boilers, and piping systems. We usually control inspection due dates through standard SAP PM maintenance plans.

However, there is a practical challenge when demonstrating compliance through reports such as IP24.

In many cases, the equipment is removed from operation before the inspection due date, which means the legal requirement is actually met on time. However, the inspection, cleaning, repairs, and maintenance activities themselves can take a significant amount of time to complete. As a consequence, the maintenance plan restart date may occur after the original due date.

From a KPI and reporting perspective, this creates inconsistencies:

during the shutdown/maintenance period, the equipment may appear as overdue in SAP, even though it was properly removed from service within the compliance window.

Today, we usually justify this situation through supporting reports and operational evidence, but this requires manual interpretation and KPI adjustments.

Has anyone else in the industry faced a similar challenge?

How do you manage compliance KPIs for equipment that is intentionally out of operation awaiting inspection or repair activities?

Also, has there ever been discussion about implementing two milestone dates within SAP PM Maintenance Plans?

For example:

- Inspection/Shutdown Start Date

- Maintenance Completion / Return-to-Service Date

I believe this would allow much more consistent compliance tracking and KPI management for regulated assets.

I would appreciate hearing how other SAP PM / Asset Management users handle this scenario.

Best regards to the SAP PM / Asset Management community.

Flávio Vargas
